Treating money differently depending on where it came from or what you mentally label it for, even though all dollars are interchangeable
A method for estimating taxes without a calculator
explanation: Mental accounting is the tendency to put money into separate mental 'buckets' (a tax refund, a bonus, 'fun money') and treat each differently, even though a dollar is a dollar. It can lead to splurging a windfall while carrying high-interest debt. Recognizing that money is fungible helps you make decisions across your whole financial picture.
source: Richard Thaler, 'Mental Accounting Matters', Journal of Behavioral Decision Making (1999)

prompt: In personal finance, what best distinguishes being 'wealthy' from merely 'looking rich'?
choices: Looking rich means owning real estate; being wealthy means renting
Looking rich is about visible spending on a lifestyle, while being wealthy is about owning assets that can sustain you without working
They mean exactly the same thing
Being wealthy requires a seven-figure salary
explanation: A high income spent on cars, travel, and a big house can make someone look rich while building little lasting wealth. Wealth is the stock of assets you have accumulated that can generate income or be spent down, independent of your paycheck. It is entirely possible to earn a lot yet be one missed paycheck from trouble.
source: Morgan Housel, 'The Psychology of Money' (2020), ch. on Wealth Is What You Don't See

prompt: Using the Rule of 114, approximately how many years will it take an investment to TRIPLE at a steady 6% annual return?
choices: 12 years
9.5 years
24 years
19 years
explanation: The Rule of 114 estimates tripling time by dividing 114 by the percentage rate: 114 / 6 = 19 years. It is a sibling of the Rule of 72 (which estimates doubling time). The true mathematical answer here is about 18.85 years, so 19 is an excellent approximation.
source: Standard finance rules-of-thumb derived from ln(3)/ln(1+r); see Investopedia, 'Rule of 72'

prompt: Using the 25× rule of thumb, what portfolio size corresponds to annual spending of $60,000?
explanation: The 25× rule says a portfolio of about 25 times your annual spending can support that spending, which mirrors a 4% withdrawal rate (since 1 / 0.04 = 25). So 25 × $60,000 = $1,500,000. The multiple comes straight from the inverse of the 4% guideline.
source: Bengen (1994) 4% rule; 25× restatement, Trinity Study tradition

prompt: A $10,000 contribution grows at 7% per year for 30 years inside a tax-deferred account where no tax is owed until withdrawal. Ignoring any withdrawal tax, what is the pre-tax balance after 30 years?
explanation: With no annual tax drag, the full balance compounds: $10,000 × 1.07^30 ≈ $76,123. The power of tax deferral is that gains compound on the entire balance each year, rather than being reduced by taxes along the way. A taxable account paying tax on distributions every year would end with meaningfully less.
source: Future-value formula FV = PV(1+r)^n; tax-deferral concept, IRS Pub 590-A

prompt: How does a 'guardrails' withdrawal strategy in retirement differ from a fixed 4%-with-inflation approach?
choices: It withdraws a constant dollar amount that never changes
It requires annuitizing the entire portfolio at retirement
It bans any withdrawals during years the market falls
It adjusts spending up or down when the withdrawal rate drifts past preset upper and lower bounds
explanation: Guardrails strategies (such as Guyton-Klinger) set trigger points: if a market drop pushes your withdrawal rate above the upper guardrail you trim spending, and if strong returns push it below the lower one you can spend more. This dynamic feedback lets retirees start with a higher initial rate than a rigid inflation-adjusted plan while reducing the risk of depletion. It does not forbid withdrawals or require annuitization.
source: Guyton & Klinger, 'Decision Rules and Maximum Initial Withdrawal Rates', Journal of Financial Planning (2006)

prompt: Assume your investments exactly keep pace with inflation (0% real return) and you target 25× your annual spending. If you save 50% of your take-home pay, how many years of saving does it take to reach your FI number?
choices: 12.5 years
50 years
25 years
17 years
explanation: Normalize income to 1 per year: you save 0.5 and spend 0.5, so your FI target is 25 × 0.5 = 12.5. With a 0% real return your savings simply accumulate, so years = 12.5 / 0.5 = 25 years. This stylized result shows that with no real growth, savings RATE alone sets the timeline; any positive real return would shorten it.
source: Mr. Money Mustache, 'The Shockingly Simple Math Behind Early Retirement' (2012)

prompt: What does a time-weighted return measure that a dollar-weighted (money-weighted) return does not?
choices: The return after subtracting all taxes and fees
The total dollars of profit earned over the period
The return the typical retail investor earns after advisor fees
The performance of the investment itself, stripped of the effect of when the investor added or withdrew cash
explanation: Time-weighted return isolates the manager's or fund's performance by neutralizing the timing and size of cash flows, which is why it is the standard for comparing funds. Dollar-weighted (internal rate of return) instead reflects the investor's actual experience, rewarding or penalizing the timing of contributions and withdrawals. The two diverge when large deposits land right before strong or weak stretches.
source: CFA Institute, GIPS standards; performance measurement (time- vs money-weighted returns)

prompt: You want to have $50,000 saved for a goal 10 years from now. Assuming a 6% annual return compounded annually, what single lump sum invested today gets you there?
explanation: Present value discounts the future target back to today: PV = $50,000 / 1.06^10 ≈ $27,920. Because returns compound over the decade, you need far less than the $50,000 face amount up front. This is the same time-value-of-money logic used to price bonds and value future cash flows.
source: Present-value formula PV = FV / (1+r)^n; standard time-value-of-money

prompt: Two savers each invest $5,000 at the END of every year and earn 7% annually. One invests for 30 years; the other waits a decade and invests for only 20 years. Approximately how much MORE does the 30-year saver have at the end?
explanation: Using the ordinary-annuity future value FV = PMT × ((1+r)^n − 1) / r: 30 years gives ≈ $472,304 and 20 years gives ≈ $204,977, a gap of about $267,326. The extra decade of contributions is small in dollars contributed but huge in compounding, illustrating the steep cost of delaying. Time in the market is the dominant lever.

prompt: Holding a 4% withdrawal rate fixed, how much LARGER must your FI portfolio be if your planned annual spending rises from $50,000 to $55,000?
explanation: At a 4% rate the required portfolio is spending / 0.04, i.e. 25× spending. So $55,000 / 0.04 − $50,000 / 0.04 = $1,375,000 − $1,250,000 = $125,000. Every extra $1 of annual spending adds $25 to the FI target, which is why trimming recurring expenses is so powerful for reaching FI.
source: 4% rule / 25× restatement; FI number = annual spending ÷ withdrawal rate

prompt: In retirement planning, what does a Monte Carlo 'success rate' of 90% mean?
choices: Your portfolio is guaranteed to grow in 90% of future years
You will spend down exactly 90% of your portfolio before death
Your annual return will be at least 9% in most years
Across many simulated return sequences, your plan avoided running out of money in 90% of them
explanation: A Monte Carlo analysis runs thousands of randomized return-and-inflation paths and reports the share in which the portfolio lasts the full horizon. A 90% success rate means the plan survived in 90% of those simulated scenarios, not that returns or growth are guaranteed. It is a probability of not depleting the portfolio, useful for stress-testing a withdrawal plan against sequence risk.
source: Monte Carlo retirement modeling; e.g., Pfau, 'How Much Can I Spend in Retirement?' (2017)

prompt: What does title insurance protect a homebuyer or lender against?
choices: Damage to the home from fire, storms, or theft after move-in
The cost of repairs for appliances that break during the first year
A drop in the home's market value after purchase
Hidden defects in the property's ownership history, such as past liens, errors, or competing claims to the title
explanation: Title insurance covers losses from problems in the chain of ownership that existed before you bought, like unpaid liens, forged deeds, or recording errors. It is usually a one-time premium paid at closing, with a lender's policy protecting the bank and an optional owner's policy protecting you. Unlike homeowners insurance, it looks backward at the title rather than forward at physical damage.
source: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, 'What is title insurance?'

prompt: How does the Closing Disclosure differ from the Loan Estimate you received earlier in the mortgage process?
choices: The Closing Disclosure shows the final, actual loan terms and costs, while the Loan Estimate was an earlier good-faith estimate of those terms
The Closing Disclosure is optional, while the Loan Estimate is legally required
The Closing Disclosure covers homeowners insurance, while the Loan Estimate covers the loan
They are the same document with two different names
explanation: The Loan Estimate is provided shortly after you apply and gives a good-faith projection of your rate, payments, and closing costs. The Closing Disclosure arrives at least three business days before closing and lists the final, confirmed numbers so you can compare them against that estimate. Reviewing the two side by side helps you catch fees that changed more than the rules allow.
source: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, 'Closing Disclosure Explainer'

prompt: What does a home warranty (home service contract) typically provide?
choices: Coverage for the structure itself against fire and storm damage
A guarantee that the home will not lose resale value
Protection against defects in the property's legal title
A service contract that helps pay to repair or replace covered appliances and home systems when they break from normal wear
explanation: A home warranty is an annual service contract that covers repair or replacement of items like the furnace, water heater, or kitchen appliances when they fail from ordinary use. It is separate from homeowners insurance, which covers sudden damage from perils like fire or theft. Buyers weigh the yearly premium and per-visit service fee against the odds and cost of the breakdowns it would cover.
source: Federal Trade Commission, consumer guidance on home warranties and service contracts

prompt: A common rule of thumb says to budget about 1% of a home's value each year for maintenance. For a $400,000 home, roughly how much per month does that imply?
choices: $40 per month
$120 per month
$333 per month
$1,000 per month
explanation: The 1% rule estimates annual upkeep as 1% of the home's value, here 0.01 x $400,000 = $4,000 per year. Dividing by 12 gives about $333 per month set aside for repairs and replacements. It is only a planning guide; older homes or deferred maintenance can push the real figure higher.
source: Common personal-finance maintenance-budgeting rule of thumb (1% of home value annually)

prompt: In a sale, the seller agrees to a concession of 3% of the $350,000 purchase price toward the buyer's closing costs. How much is that concession in dollars?
explanation: A seller concession is money the seller credits toward the buyer's closing costs, expressed here as a percentage of price: 0.03 x $350,000 = $10,500. This reduces the cash the buyer needs at closing without lowering the loan amount. Loan programs cap how large concessions can be relative to the price.
source: Standard percentage calculation of a seller concession on purchase price

prompt: What is a key difference between an FHA loan and a conventional loan?
choices: FHA loans are government-insured and often allow lower down payments and credit scores, while conventional loans are not government-insured and typically have stricter qualifying standards
FHA loans can only be used for second homes, while conventional loans are only for primary residences
FHA loans never charge mortgage insurance, while conventional loans always do
FHA loans always carry a higher interest rate than any conventional loan
explanation: FHA loans are insured by the Federal Housing Administration, which lets lenders accept smaller down payments and lower credit scores, making them popular with first-time buyers. Conventional loans are not government-insured and generally require stronger credit and larger down payments to avoid extra costs. FHA loans carry their own mortgage insurance premiums, which can be harder to remove than conventional PMI.
source: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development overview of FHA-insured mortgages

prompt: In many U.S. states, what does a property-tax homestead exemption do for an owner-occupied primary residence?
choices: It exempts the homeowner from paying any property tax for the first year of ownership
It shields a portion of the home's assessed value from taxation, lowering the taxable base used to calculate property tax
It freezes the home's market value so it can never be reassessed
It transfers the property-tax bill from the owner to the mortgage lender
explanation: A homestead exemption reduces the assessed value subject to property tax on a qualifying primary residence, so the tax is calculated on a smaller base. The exact dollar or percentage reduction and eligibility rules vary widely by state and locality. Some states layer on additional protections, such as caps on annual assessment increases or creditor protection, but the core property-tax effect is a lower taxable value.
source: State and local property-tax homestead exemption statutes (general U.S. overview)

prompt: Which statement best describes a VA loan for eligible veterans and service members?
choices: It is a grant that does not have to be repaid
It is a short-term bridge loan that must be refinanced within one year
It is funded directly by the Department of Veterans Affairs rather than by private lenders
It is a mortgage guaranteed in part by the Department of Veterans Affairs that often requires no down payment and charges no monthly mortgage insurance
explanation: VA loans are made by private lenders but partly guaranteed by the Department of Veterans Affairs, which reduces lender risk and lets many borrowers buy with no down payment. They do not charge ongoing monthly mortgage insurance, though most borrowers pay a one-time VA funding fee that can be financed. Eligibility depends on military service requirements and obtaining a Certificate of Eligibility.
source: U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs, VA-guaranteed home loan program overview

prompt: How does an HOA special assessment differ from regular HOA dues?
choices: It is a one-time or temporary charge levied to fund a specific large expense, separate from the recurring regular dues
It is a discount the HOA gives owners when reserves are full
It is the portion of regular dues set aside for the management company's salary
It is a penalty paid only by owners who rent out their units
explanation: Regular dues are predictable, recurring payments that fund the association's ongoing operating budget. A special assessment is an extra charge imposed when reserves fall short of a major cost, such as a new roof, elevator, or unexpected repair. Because special assessments can be large and arrive with little notice, buyers often review an HOA's reserve funding and assessment history before purchasing.
source: General homeowners-association governance and reserve-funding practices

prompt: The 5% rule estimates the annual unrecoverable cost of owning as 5% of a home's value (property tax, maintenance, and cost of capital combined). For a $500,000 home, what monthly rent would roughly break even under this rule?
choices: $1,250 per month
$2,083 per month
$2,500 per month
$4,167 per month
explanation: The 5% rule multiplies the home's value by 5% to approximate yearly unrecoverable ownership costs: 0.05 x $500,000 = $25,000 per year. Dividing by 12 gives about $2,083 per month, the rent below which renting is often cheaper than buying that home. It is a rough screen and ignores appreciation, leverage, and local specifics.
source: Ben Felix's 5% rule for the rent-versus-buy decision (unrecoverable cost of ownership)

prompt: You agree to buy a home for $420,000, but the lender's appraisal comes in at $400,000. Since the loan is based on the lower value, how large is the appraisal gap you would need to cover in cash to keep the deal together?
explanation: An appraisal gap is the shortfall when the appraised value is below the contract price: $420,000 - $400,000 = $20,000. Lenders size the mortgage off the lower appraised value, so the buyer must either bring the extra cash, renegotiate the price, or walk away if the contract allows. An appraisal-gap clause is a buyer's promise to cover some or all of this difference.
source: Standard appraisal-gap calculation (contract price minus appraised value)

prompt: In a jurisdiction that reassesses a home to its sale price at purchase, you buy for $500,000 in an area with a 1.2% effective property-tax rate. If the prior owner was taxed on an assessed value of $350,000, how much does the annual property-tax bill increase after your purchase?
explanation: Reassessment resets the taxable value to your purchase price, so your tax becomes 0.012 x $500,000 = $6,000, versus the prior 0.012 x $350,000 = $4,200. The increase is $6,000 - $4,200 = $1,800 per year. Buyers in such areas should budget from the reassessed value rather than the seller's old tax bill, which can understate the true cost.

prompt: What does a dividend reinvestment plan (DRIP) do?
choices: It lets you cash out a fund's dividends early, before the official payment date, for a small fee.
It guarantees a fixed dividend rate that cannot be cut even if the company's profits fall.
It automatically uses the cash dividends a holding pays to buy more shares of that same investment.
It converts your dividends into bonds issued by the same company to reduce your stock risk.
explanation: A DRIP automatically takes the cash dividends an investment pays and uses them to buy more shares of that same holding, often including fractional shares. This compounds your position over time without you having to act, and many brokers offer it for free. Note the dividends are still taxable in a taxable account even though you never see the cash.
source: U.S. SEC Investor.gov — Dividend Reinvestment Plans

prompt: What is a fund's prospectus?
choices: A marketing brochure that lists only a fund's best historical years to attract new investors.
The official disclosure document describing a fund's objectives, strategy, risks, fees, and past performance.
A monthly account statement showing the current value of the shares you personally own.
A contract that locks your money in the fund for a fixed number of years before you can sell.
explanation: A prospectus is the formal disclosure document a fund must publish, laying out its investment objective, strategy, risks, fees and expenses, and historical performance. Reading it (or at least the short 'summary prospectus') is the standard way to understand what you're buying before you invest. It is filed with the SEC, not a sales brochure.
source: U.S. SEC Investor.gov — Mutual Fund Prospectus

prompt: What is a robo-advisor?
choices: A chatbot that gives you specific buy and sell tips on individual stocks in real time.
A high-frequency trading bot that tries to profit from tiny price moves within seconds.
A service that lets you copy the exact trades of a famous professional investor automatically.
A digital service that builds and automatically manages a diversified portfolio for you based on your goals and risk tolerance.
explanation: A robo-advisor is an automated online service that picks a diversified portfolio (usually low-cost index funds) for you based on answers about your goals, time horizon, and risk tolerance, then handles rebalancing and sometimes tax-loss harvesting. It typically charges a small annual advisory fee on top of the underlying funds' costs. It is built around long-term allocation, not stock-picking tips.

prompt: What are fractional shares?
choices: Shares that pay only a fraction of the dividend a normal share pays.
The ability to buy less than one whole share, so you can invest a set dollar amount even in a pricey stock.
Shares that you are only allowed to sell in fractions over several years.
Discounted shares a company sells to employees at a fraction of the market price.
explanation: Fractional shares let you buy a portion of a single share, so you can invest a specific dollar amount even when one share costs hundreds of dollars. This makes it easy to put your whole intended contribution to work and to diversify with a small balance. Your fraction still earns its proportional share of any dividends and price change.
source: U.S. SEC Investor.gov — Fractional Share Investing

prompt: What is the difference between a market order and a limit order?
choices: A market order can only be placed while the market is open; a limit order can only be placed after hours.
A market order buys index funds; a limit order buys individual stocks.
A market order is for selling and a limit order is for buying — they cannot be swapped.
A market order executes immediately at the best available price; a limit order executes only at your specified price or better.
explanation: A market order tells your broker to trade right away at whatever the current best price is, prioritizing speed of execution. A limit order sets a price you are willing to accept and only fills at that price or better, prioritizing price control but with no guarantee it executes. Both can be used to buy or sell.
source: U.S. SEC Investor.gov — Types of Orders

prompt: What is a REIT (real estate investment trust)?
choices: A company that owns or finances income-producing real estate, letting you invest in property by buying its shares.
A government program that helps first-time buyers afford a down payment on a home.
A type of mortgage that lets you borrow against the equity in your house.
A savings account specifically earmarked for buying a rental property someday.
explanation: A REIT is a company that owns, operates, or finances income-producing real estate such as apartments, offices, or warehouses, and you invest by buying its shares like a stock. REITs must pay out most of their taxable income as dividends, so they tend to have high yields. They let you get real-estate exposure without buying or managing property yourself.
source: U.S. SEC Investor.gov — Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs)

prompt: What is a bond ladder?
choices: A strategy of selling a bond every time interest rates tick up to lock in a quick gain.
A single long-term bond that pays a higher coupon the longer you hold it.
Buying bonds that all mature on the same date so you get one big payout.
Holding several bonds with staggered maturity dates so a portion comes due at regular intervals.
explanation: A bond ladder holds multiple bonds whose maturity dates are spread out (for example, maturing in 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 years) so a portion of the money becomes available at regular intervals. As each rung matures you can reinvest at current rates, which smooths out interest-rate risk and provides predictable cash flow. It avoids betting everything on one maturity or one moment in the rate cycle.

prompt: How do 'growth' stocks and 'value' stocks generally differ as investment styles?
choices: Growth stocks pay high dividends while value stocks never pay any dividends at all.
Growth stocks are expected to expand earnings rapidly and trade at high valuations, while value stocks trade cheaply relative to fundamentals like earnings or book value.
Growth stocks are always large companies and value stocks are always small companies.
Growth stocks are guaranteed by the issuing company while value stocks carry no such guarantee.
explanation: Growth stocks are companies the market expects to grow earnings quickly, so investors pay a premium and they carry high price-to-earnings or price-to-book ratios. Value stocks trade at low prices relative to fundamentals like earnings, book value, or cash flow, often because they are out of favor. The two styles tend to lead at different points in the market cycle, which is why some investors hold both.

prompt: An investor targets 60% stocks and rebalances only when the stock weight drifts more than 5 percentage points from target. On a $100,000 portfolio, at what stock dollar value does the upper rebalancing band trigger?
explanation: $65,000. With a 60% target and a 5-percentage-point tolerance band, the upper trigger is 60% + 5% = 65% of the portfolio. On $100,000 that is 0.65 × $100,000 = $65,000, so once stocks grow past $65,000 the investor rebalances. Tolerance bands cut down on needless trading by only acting when the drift is meaningful.

prompt: A stock has a beta of 1.5 relative to the market. Based on beta alone, if the overall market rises 8% in a period, what is the stock's expected move?
explanation: 12%. Beta measures how much a stock tends to move relative to the market: an expected move of beta × market return. Here 1.5 × 8% = 12%, so a beta of 1.5 implies the stock is roughly 50% more volatile than the market. Beta describes systematic (market) risk only and is an average tendency, not a guarantee for any single period.

prompt: How does the Sortino ratio differ from the Sharpe ratio?
choices: The Sortino ratio ignores the risk-free rate entirely, while the Sharpe ratio subtracts it.
The Sortino ratio measures return per unit of fees, while the Sharpe ratio measures return per unit of risk.
The Sortino ratio penalizes only downside (harmful) volatility, while the Sharpe ratio penalizes total volatility including upside swings.
The Sortino ratio applies only to bonds, while the Sharpe ratio applies only to stocks.
explanation: Both ratios divide excess return (return above the risk-free rate) by a measure of risk, but they define risk differently. The Sharpe ratio uses total volatility, counting big upside moves as 'risk' too, while the Sortino ratio uses only downside deviation — the volatility of returns that fall below a target. Because investors usually don't mind upside surprises, the Sortino ratio is often seen as a more focused gauge of harmful risk.

prompt: What is a key difference between working as a 1099 independent contractor and a W-2 employee?
choices: A 1099 contractor is always paid more per hour than any W-2 employee
A W-2 employee can never receive any workplace benefits
A 1099 contractor is legally required to incorporate a business first
A W-2 employee has income and payroll taxes withheld by the employer, while a 1099 contractor receives gross pay and is responsible for their own taxes
explanation: A W-2 employee has federal/state income tax and the employee share of FICA withheld from each paycheck, and the employer pays the matching half of FICA. A 1099 independent contractor is paid the full amount with nothing withheld and must handle income tax plus self-employment tax (both halves of Social Security and Medicare) themselves. Contractors also typically lack employer benefits and protections like overtime.

prompt: Why do many self-employed people and independent contractors pay quarterly estimated taxes?
choices: Because no income tax is withheld from their pay, so the IRS expects tax to be paid in installments throughout the year
Because the IRS forbids self-employed people from filing an annual return
Because quarterly payments are a way to permanently lower their total tax rate
Because estimated taxes replace the need to ever file a Form 1040
explanation: The U.S. tax system is pay-as-you-go. Employees meet this through paycheck withholding, but the self-employed have nothing withheld, so they send estimated payments roughly four times a year to cover income tax and self-employment tax. Skipping or underpaying these can trigger an underpayment penalty even if you pay in full at filing time.

prompt: What is a commuter (transit) benefit offered by some employers?
choices: A program that lets you set aside pre-tax pay to cover qualified transit or parking commuting costs
A cash bonus paid only to employees who drive to work
Free company housing located next to the office
A tax credit that eliminates your entire commute cost at filing time
explanation: A commuter benefit lets you fund qualified transit passes, vanpooling, or workplace parking with pre-tax dollars up to a monthly IRS limit, lowering your taxable income. Because the money is deducted before taxes, it reduces what you pay in income and payroll taxes on those commuting costs. It is a use-it-or-lose-it style benefit tied to actual commuting expenses, not a cash payment.

prompt: What is employer tuition reimbursement (educational assistance) as a workplace benefit?
choices: A loan from your employer that you must repay with interest after graduation
An employer program that pays for or refunds part of your eligible education costs, often up to an annual limit
A government grant paid directly to students that bypasses the employer
A requirement that employers cover 100% of any degree an employee chooses
explanation: Tuition reimbursement (educational assistance) is a benefit where an employer pays for or refunds eligible course or degree costs, frequently capped at an annual dollar amount and sometimes conditioned on grades or staying with the company. Under current IRS rules a certain amount of employer educational assistance per year can be received tax-free. It is a benefit, not a loan, though some plans require you to repay it if you leave soon after.

prompt: Your employer wants you to keep $10,000 net from a relocation bonus after a flat 30% supplemental tax withholding is applied. To 'gross up' the payment so you net $10,000, what gross bonus must the employer pay? (Round to the nearest dollar.)
choices: $14,286 gross, because gross = net / (1 - tax rate) = $10,000 / 0.70
$13,000 gross, by adding 30% of the net amount on top
$10,000 gross, since the bonus is already the target amount
$12,000 gross, by adding 20% of the net amount on top
explanation: Grossing up means solving net = gross x (1 - rate) for the gross. With a 30% withholding rate, gross = $10,000 / (1 - 0.30) = $10,000 / 0.70 = $14,285.71, about $14,286. Simply adding 30% of the net ($13,000) is too low, because that 30% is itself partly taxed. The extra dollars cover the tax on the bonus so your take-home lands at the intended $10,000.

prompt: Your employer matches 50% of your 401(k) contributions on the first 6% of your $80,000 salary, and you contribute enough to capture the full match. In dollars, how much is that annual match worth (which equals an effective 3% raise on your salary)?
choices: $4,800, equal to the full 6% of salary you contribute
$1,200, counting only half of half the match
$7,200, adding your 6% and the match together
$2,400, because the match equals 50% x 6% x $80,000, or 3% of salary
explanation: The employer adds 50 cents per dollar on the first 6% of pay, so the match is 0.50 x 0.06 x $80,000 = $2,400. As a share of salary that is $2,400 / $80,000 = 3%, effectively a 3% raise you only receive if you contribute at least 6%. Failing to contribute up to the match leaves this guaranteed return on the table.
source: 401(k) employer match expressed as effective compensation

prompt: 300 RSUs vest when the share price is $40, and your employer uses 'sell-to-cover' at the 22% statutory supplemental withholding rate to pay the taxes. Rounding up to whole shares, how many shares are sold to cover the tax withholding?
choices: 66 shares, because tax = 22% x (300 x $40) = $2,640, and $2,640 / $40 = 66 shares
22 shares, applying the 22% rate directly to the share count
300 shares, since the entire grant is sold at vest
30 shares, withholding a flat 10% of the shares
explanation: At vesting the RSUs are taxed as ordinary income on their fair market value: 300 x $40 = $12,000. The withholding is 22% x $12,000 = $2,640, and sell-to-cover liquidates just enough shares to raise that cash: $2,640 / $40 = 66 shares. You keep the remaining 234 shares, and the $2,640 is remitted as tax withholding on your behalf.
source: RSU vesting and sell-to-cover withholding mechanics

prompt: A non-exempt employee is paid a fixed salary of $1,000 for a 40-hour workweek but works 45 hours one week. Using the regular rate derived from the salary, what is that week's total gross pay with time-and-a-half owed on hours over 40?
choices: $1,187.50, because the regular rate is $1,000 / 40 = $25/hr and 5 OT hours x $25 x 1.5 = $187.50 on top of the $1,000
$1,000.00, since a salaried employee is never owed overtime
$1,150.00, paying the 5 extra hours at the straight $25 rate plus a small bonus
$1,500.00, paying all 45 hours at time-and-a-half
explanation: Being salaried does not make a worker exempt; non-exempt employees must receive overtime regardless of how they are paid. The regular rate here is $1,000 / 40 = $25/hour, so the 5 hours over 40 are paid at 1.5 x $25 = $37.50 each, adding $187.50. Total gross is $1,000 + $187.50 = $1,187.50.
source: U.S. DOL FLSA overtime rules for non-exempt salaried employees

prompt: What is the basic difference between 'garden leave' and a non-compete clause?
choices: Garden leave and a non-compete are two names for the exact same agreement
During garden leave you stay employed and paid but are kept away from work during a notice period, whereas a non-compete restricts where you may work after employment ends
Garden leave pays you to work for a competitor, while a non-compete pays you to stay home
Both apply only to hourly workers and are illegal for salaried employees
explanation: Under garden leave you remain an employee on payroll during a notice period but are told not to come to work or contact clients, which keeps you idle and out of a competitor's hands while still bound by duties to your current employer. A non-compete is a post-employment restriction that limits working for competitors for a defined time and area after you leave, and is typically unpaid. Enforceability of non-competes varies widely by state and has faced regulatory challenges.
source: Employment law overview: garden leave vs. non-compete clauses

prompt: A spouse who is legally changing their last name after marriage wants to update their financial accounts smoothly. Which sequence usually causes the fewest problems?
choices: Update your credit cards first, since merchants check those most often
Change the name on your bank accounts before doing anything with the government
Tell your employer's payroll team first so your paychecks keep clearing
Update Social Security first, then your driver's license and passport, then banks, cards, and payroll
explanation: The name on file with the Social Security Administration is the anchor that other institutions and the IRS reconcile against, so updating it first prevents mismatches that can freeze tax refunds or new accounts. After the SSA card, you update government photo IDs (driver's license, passport), then financial accounts like banks, credit cards, and payroll. Doing banks or cards before the SSA record risks a name that doesn't match the IRS or your ID.
source: Social Security Administration, 'Changing Your Name' (ssa.gov); USA.gov name-change checklist

prompt: A couple who just bought a home is shopping for insurance and hears they can get a 'bundling' or 'multi-policy' discount. What does that mean?
choices: Buying auto and homeowners insurance from the same company, which often lowers the price on both
Combining several months of premiums into one annual payment to skip monthly fees
Adding extra coverage riders so a single policy covers more types of loss
Switching from monthly to paperless billing to qualify for an online-only rate
explanation: A multi-policy or 'bundling' discount is what insurers offer when you carry more than one policy with them, most commonly auto plus homeowners (or renters), and it typically reduces the premium on each. Insurers reward bundling because multi-policy customers tend to stay longer and are cheaper to service. It is different from paying annually or adding riders, which change how or how much you pay but not the multi-policy discount itself.
source: NAIC consumer guidance on shopping for home and auto insurance (naic.org)

prompt: A couple has saved $30,000 and is debating whether to spend it on a wedding or put it toward a house. If a lender requires a 10% down payment, what is the most expensive home price their $30,000 could cover as the down payment instead?
choices: $33,000, because a wedding usually costs about 10% more than expected
$150,000, because lenders cap the loan at five times the down payment
$270,000, the home price minus their full down payment
$300,000, since a 10% down payment is one-tenth of the purchase price
explanation: A 10% down payment equals one-tenth of the purchase price, so the largest price the down payment can cover is the down payment divided by 0.10: $30,000 / 0.10 = $300,000. This framing shows the opportunity cost of a large wedding — the same cash could anchor a sizable home purchase. (A real budget would also need closing costs and reserves, which this simplified comparison sets aside.)
source: Standard loan-to-value arithmetic; CFPB homebuyer resources (consumerfinance.gov)

prompt: New parents estimate the baby will add three recurring monthly costs to their budget: $1,100 for daycare, $250 for diapers, formula, and supplies, and $150 for the larger family health-insurance premium. How much will these add to their spending over the baby's first 12 months?
choices: $1,500, since that is the new total they pay each month
$16,200, counting only daycare and diapers for the year
$18,000, the combined monthly increase multiplied by 12 months
$22,200, the monthly costs plus a one-time $4,200 of baby gear
explanation: The monthly budget delta is $1,100 + $250 + $150 = $1,500, and over a full year that is $1,500 x 12 = $18,000. The $1,500 choice is the monthly figure, not the annual one, and the others add or omit costs not given. Seeing the first-year delta as a single number helps parents size their emergency fund and cash-flow plan before the baby arrives.
source: Illustrative budgeting arithmetic; USDA expenditures-on-children framework

prompt: A family that adopted a child is told they may claim the federal adoption tax credit. Which statement best describes how this credit generally works?
choices: It is a refundable credit, so any amount beyond your tax bill is paid out to you as a refund
It is a deduction that reduces your taxable income by the amount of your adoption expenses
It only applies to international adoptions and never to adopting a U.S. child from foster care
It is a nonrefundable credit for qualified adoption expenses, capped per child, that phases out at higher incomes and can be carried forward if it exceeds your tax for the year
explanation: The adoption tax credit offsets qualified adoption expenses up to a per-child maximum, but it is nonrefundable — it can reduce your tax to zero, and any unused portion carries forward for up to five years rather than being paid out. It phases out for higher-income taxpayers and applies to domestic (including special-needs/foster) and international adoptions, not just foreign ones. (The exact dollar cap and phase-out range are indexed and change yearly, so plan on the structure, not a fixed figure.)
source: IRS Topic No. 607, Adoption Credit and Adoption Assistance Programs (irs.gov)

prompt: An adult child is starting to plan for an aging parent's potential long-term care. Which assumption most often derails these financial plans?
choices: Assuming Medicare will pay for extended custodial long-term care, when it generally covers only limited skilled care, not ongoing help with daily living
Assuming home modifications are tax-deductible, when they reduce the parent's property tax instead
Assuming a reverse mortgage must be repaid monthly, when it is always interest-free
Assuming Social Security automatically increases to cover a parent's care costs
explanation: A common and costly misconception is that Medicare covers long-term custodial care; in fact Medicare pays only for limited, medically necessary skilled care (such as short rehab stays), not ongoing help with bathing, dressing, or eating. Extended custodial care is typically paid out of pocket, through long-term-care insurance, or via Medicaid once assets are largely spent down. Planning early for these costs — and for the caregiver's own lost wages and time — avoids a crisis when care is suddenly needed.

prompt: In the weeks after a spouse or parent dies, which action is widely recommended as an early financial step — before rushing to close accounts or pay every bill?
choices: Immediately cancel the deceased's Social Security number with the IRS so no taxes are owed
Pay off all of the deceased's debts from your own personal funds right away to avoid collections
Move every asset out of the deceased's name within 30 days or it is forfeited to the state
Obtain multiple certified copies of the death certificate, since banks, insurers, and government agencies each require one to act
explanation: Certified death certificates are the gateway document — banks, life insurers, brokerage firms, Social Security, and the DMV each typically require an original certified copy, so ordering 10 or more up front prevents weeks of delay. Survivors generally should NOT pay the deceased's debts from personal funds; those debts are paid by the estate, and heirs are usually not personally liable. There is no rule that assets are forfeited in 30 days, and the SSA (not the survivor) handles benefit and record updates.
source: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, guidance for managing a deceased person's accounts (consumerfinance.gov); SSA survivors information

prompt: Grandparents want to make a large one-time contribution to a grandchild's 529 plan without using any of their lifetime gift-tax exemption. What does the special 529 '5-year election' (sometimes called superfunding) let them do?
choices: Treat a lump sum of up to five years' worth of the annual gift exclusion as if it were spread evenly over five years for gift-tax purposes
Withdraw the contribution tax-free after five years regardless of how the money is spent
Lock in this year's investment returns for five years so the account cannot lose value
Deduct the entire contribution on their federal income tax return over the next five years
explanation: A unique 529 provision lets a donor elect to treat a single large gift as if made evenly over five years, so a front-loaded lump sum up to five times the annual exclusion can avoid gift tax and preserve the lifetime exemption — powerful for jump-starting compounding. It is a gift-tax timing election (reported on Form 709), not a federal income-tax deduction, and it does not change the withdrawal rules or guarantee returns. Dying within the five years pulls a prorated portion back into the estate.
source: IRS Publication 970 and Form 709 instructions; Internal Revenue Code section 529(c)(2)(B)

prompt: Someone planning a 6-month unpaid career break has essential living expenses of $4,000 per month and wants to also set aside a $5,000 cushion for surprises. Ignoring any income during the break, what total should they save before starting it?
choices: $24,000, which covers the six months of expenses only
$29,000, the six months of essential expenses plus the cushion
$9,000, one month of expenses plus the cushion
$48,000, a full year of expenses to be safe
explanation: The target is the monthly burn rate times the number of months, plus the buffer: $4,000 x 6 + $5,000 = $29,000. The $24,000 option omits the cushion, and the $48,000 option funds twelve months rather than the six-month break described. Funding the break in cash beforehand keeps the person from raiding retirement accounts or taking on debt while their income is paused.
source: Illustrative cash-runway arithmetic; standard emergency-fund/sabbatical planning guidance

prompt: You keep your savings at a credit union instead of a bank. Which agency insures your deposits there?
choices: The SEC, the same agency that oversees stocks and brokerage accounts
No one — only banks can insure deposits, so credit-union money is uninsured
The FDIC, the exact same fund that backs bank deposits
The NCUA, which insures credit-union shares up to $250,000 — the same limit the FDIC gives banks
explanation: Federally insured credit unions are backed by the National Credit Union Administration (NCUA) through the National Credit Union Share Insurance Fund, not the FDIC. Coverage mirrors bank protection at $250,000 per depositor, per institution, per ownership category. The two funds are separate but offer equivalent, full-faith-and-credit U.S. government backing.

prompt: What is TreasuryDirect?
choices: A high-yield savings account offered by large commercial banks
The U.S. government's official website for buying savings bonds and Treasury securities directly, with no broker or fee
A federal program that automatically deposits your tax refund into a CD
A private app that rounds up purchases and invests the change in index funds
explanation: TreasuryDirect is the U.S. Treasury's online platform where individuals can buy and hold savings bonds (I-bonds and EE-bonds) and marketable Treasuries like bills, notes, and bonds straight from the government. Because there is no middleman, there are no purchase fees or commissions. Holdings are registered electronically rather than as paper certificates.
source: TreasuryDirect.gov — U.S. Department of the Treasury

prompt: Your brokerage offers a 'cash management account' (CMA) alongside your investments. What is it?
choices: A retirement account with special tax breaks for cash savings
A margin loan that lets you borrow against your stocks at a low rate
An account that automatically buys index funds with any idle cash
A cash account at a brokerage that blends checking-like features — debit card, bill pay — with interest, often sweeping balances to partner banks for FDIC coverage
explanation: A cash management account is offered by a brokerage or fintech, not a traditional bank, and bundles everyday spending features (debit card, bill pay, ATM access) with interest on your balance. Many CMAs obtain FDIC insurance by sweeping deposits to one or more partner banks behind the scenes. They let you keep spending cash close to your investments without a separate bank.
source: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau — deposit and cash account basics

prompt: What is the special guarantee that comes with a U.S. Series EE savings bond?
choices: It pays a rate that rises with the stock market each year
The Treasury guarantees it will at least double in value if you hold it for 20 years
It can be cashed any day in the first year with no penalty
It is exempt from federal income tax when redeemed
explanation: The Treasury guarantees that a Series EE bond will be worth at least double its purchase price after 20 years; if the bond's regular interest hasn't gotten it there, the Treasury makes a one-time adjustment to double it. That makes the 20-year hold the key to the guarantee. Cashing out before 5 years forfeits the last 3 months of interest, and EE interest is federally taxable but exempt from state and local tax.
source: TreasuryDirect — Series EE savings bond features

prompt: When comparing money-market mutual funds, the headline number you'll usually see quoted is the '7-day yield.' What does it tell you?
choices: The total return the fund earned over the past seven years
A guaranteed rate the fund promises to pay for the next seven days
The fund's most recent week of income, annualized — a standardized snapshot of its current yield
How many days it takes to withdraw your money from the fund
explanation: The 7-day yield takes the income a money-market fund distributed over the past seven days, nets out fees, and annualizes it into a comparable yearly rate. Because these funds reset constantly, it is the standard, near-current measure regulators require for apples-to-apples comparison. It is not a guarantee — the rate floats as short-term interest rates move.
source: SEC — money market fund yield disclosure rules

prompt: Many planners suggest building an emergency fund in tiers — for example a small starter amount first, then one month of expenses, then three to six months. Why build it in stages?
choices: Reaching an early, achievable milestone protects you from small shocks and builds momentum before you finish the full fund
Saving in stages lets you skip paying taxes on the interest
Each tier must be held at a different bank to stay FDIC-insured
Tiers are required by law before you can open a savings account
explanation: A tiered or 'laddered' emergency fund sets reachable checkpoints — such as a $1,000 starter cushion, then one month of expenses, then the full three-to-six-month target — so you're protected against minor surprises long before the whole fund is done. Hitting early milestones provides motivation and keeps you from raiding the fund. The stages are a behavioral strategy, not a tax or insurance rule.
source: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau — emergency savings guidance

prompt: A saver wants to know how NCUA share insurance at a credit union compares with FDIC insurance at a bank. Which statement is accurate?
choices: NCUA covers only $100,000, half of the FDIC limit
NCUA insurance is privately funded and lacks U.S. government backing
FDIC and NCUA coverage can be stacked at the same institution for double protection
Both insure $250,000 per owner, per institution, per ownership category, and both carry full faith and credit of the U.S. government
explanation: The NCUA's Share Insurance Fund and the FDIC both protect $250,000 per depositor, per institution, per ownership category, and both are backed by the full faith and credit of the United States. The practical difference is the charter: FDIC covers banks, NCUA covers federally insured credit unions. You cannot stack both at one institution, but spreading deposits across a bank and a credit union gives you separate $250,000 limits at each.
source: NCUA and FDIC deposit insurance comparison

prompt: A Series I savings bond has a fixed rate of 1.30% and a semiannual inflation rate of 1.97%. Using the Treasury composite-rate formula, fixed + (2 × semiannual inflation) + (fixed × semiannual inflation), what is the composite (annual) rate?
choices: 3.27%, found by simply adding the fixed rate to the semiannual inflation rate
1.97%, because only the inflation component matters
5.27%, combining the fixed rate with twice the semiannual inflation plus the small cross-term
3.94%, which is just twice the semiannual inflation rate
explanation: The Treasury composite rate is fixed + 2×(semiannual inflation) + (fixed × semiannual inflation) = 0.0130 + 2(0.0197) + (0.0130)(0.0197) ≈ 0.0527, or about 5.27%. The semiannual inflation rate is doubled to approximate an annual rate, and the small cross-term accounts for the fixed rate compounding on top of inflation. The fixed portion stays for the life of the bond while the inflation portion resets every six months.

prompt: A Series EE bond is guaranteed to double in value if held for 20 years. What effective annualized return does that doubling guarantee imply?
choices: 5.00% per year, since doubling means 100% total divided by 20 years
3.53% per year, the rate at which money doubles in exactly 20 years
7.20% per year, from the Rule of 72 applied to 10 years
2.00% per year, one-tenth of the total gain
explanation: Doubling over 20 years means (1 + r)^20 = 2, so r = 2^(1/20) − 1 ≈ 0.0353, or about 3.53% compounded annually. Dividing 100% by 20 ignores compounding and overstates the rate. This guaranteed floor is why EE bonds are most attractive when held the full 20 years.

prompt: You have $25,000 to build a 5-year CD ladder with one CD (rung) maturing each year. If you size the rungs equally, how much goes into each CD?
choices: $12,500, splitting the money between just the first and last rungs
$2,500, putting 10% into each rung
$25,000 in the 5-year CD, since longer terms pay the most
$5,000 per rung, dividing the total evenly across the five maturities
explanation: An equally weighted ladder divides the total by the number of rungs: $25,000 ÷ 5 = $5,000 in each CD. As each rung matures, you reinvest it into a new long-term CD at the top of the ladder, keeping one CD coming due every year. Equal sizing gives steady, predictable access to a portion of your cash on a regular schedule.
source: CD ladder construction: total ÷ number of rungs

prompt: A money-market fund lists both a '7-day SEC yield' and a '30-day SEC yield.' What is the practical difference between them?
choices: The 7-day yield is after taxes while the 30-day yield is before taxes
The 7-day yield reflects the most recent week annualized, while the 30-day yield averages a longer window and reacts more slowly to rate changes
The 30-day yield is guaranteed for the next month while the 7-day yield is not
They are identical figures presented in two formats
explanation: Both are standardized, fee-adjusted yields annualized from recent income, but they use different lookback windows. The 7-day yield captures the latest week, so it moves quickly when short-term rates shift; the 30-day yield smooths over a month and lags. In a rising-rate environment the 7-day yield typically reads higher than the 30-day, and the reverse when rates are falling. Neither is a guarantee of future yield.
source: SEC — standardized money market fund yield definitions

prompt: A cash management account advertises 'up to $2 million in FDIC insurance' even though the standard limit is $250,000 per bank. How is that possible?
choices: The brokerage privately guarantees the extra coverage out of its own capital
FDIC limits do not apply to brokerage accounts, so any amount is covered
It sweeps your cash across multiple partner banks, so each bank holds an FDIC-insured slice under the $250,000 limit
The SEC, not the FDIC, insures the balance up to $2 million
explanation: The cash is not held by the brokerage itself — it is swept into deposit accounts at a network of partner banks. Because FDIC coverage is per depositor, per bank, spreading the balance across many banks lets each $250,000 slice be separately insured, multiplying total protection. The expanded coverage depends on the program's bank list, and holding funds directly at one of those same banks could reduce your available limit there.
source: FDIC insurance and brokerage cash-sweep program disclosures

prompt: Your essential expenses are $3,500 a month and you want a 4-month emergency fund. You'll keep one month of it in checking for instant access and put the remaining months in a high-yield savings account. How much goes into the HYSA?
choices: $14,000, the full four months, with nothing kept in checking
$7,000, exactly half of the fund
$3,500, only the single month meant for checking
$10,500, the three months that sit beyond the one-month checking buffer
explanation: The full 4-month fund is 4 × $3,500 = $14,000. Keeping one month ($3,500) in checking leaves $14,000 − $3,500 = $10,500 for the HYSA. Splitting the fund this way keeps a small buffer instantly available for spending while the larger share earns a higher yield, balancing liquidity against return.
source: Tiered emergency-fund allocation: (months × expenses) − checking buffer

prompt: What is a common trap of "buy now, pay later" (BNPL) services that split a purchase into interest-free installments?
choices: They report every on-time payment to credit bureaus, which lowers your score
They make spending feel painless, so people stack multiple plans and rack up late fees they can't track
They are illegal in most U.S. states and can void your purchase
They always charge a higher price than paying in cash up front
explanation: Splitting a cost into four small "interest-free" payments makes a purchase feel cheaper and easier to justify, which encourages buying more and juggling several overlapping plans at once. The danger is losing track of the staggered due dates: a missed payment triggers late fees, and some plans flip into steep deferred interest. The installments themselves may be free, but the behavioral nudge to overspend and the penalties for slipping up are the real cost.
source: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau — Buy Now, Pay Later market report (2022)

prompt: Auditing your recurring charges, you cancel three subscriptions you no longer use: $15.99, $9.99, and $12.49 per month. How much do you save over a full year?
choices: $38.47, the one-month total
$230.82, counting only half the year
$461.64 per year
$153.88, multiplying by four
explanation: Recurring charges repeat every month, so annual savings = monthly total × 12. The three subscriptions add to 15.99 + 9.99 + 12.49 = $38.47 per month, and $38.47 × 12 = $461.64 per year. A single ten-minute audit of forgotten subscriptions can free up real money because the saving recurs every month and into future years.

prompt: A family-size box of pasta costs $7.20 and makes 8 servings. What is its price per serving?
choices: $0.90 per serving
$1.20 per serving
$7.20 per serving
$0.72 per serving
explanation: Price per serving is the total price divided by the number of servings: 7.20 ÷ 8 = $0.90 per serving. Comparing price per serving — rather than the price on the front of the package — lets you judge value fairly across different package sizes and recipes. The $0.72 distractor wrongly divides by 10 servings, and $1.20 divides by 6.

prompt: You buy a $4.50 energy drink every single day. Ignoring any investing, what does this daily habit cost you over one year (365 days)?
choices: $162.00, counting only weekdays
$1,642.50 per year
$54.00, just one month
$135.00, just one month of daily buys
explanation: A daily habit's yearly cost is the per-day price times the number of days: 4.50 × 365 = $1,642.50 a year. Putting a recurring small purchase on an annual basis often reveals a number far larger than the trivial daily price suggests, which is the whole point of tracking these little leaks. This is the simple annualized cost; investing the money instead would make the forgone amount even larger.

prompt: What is the "1% rule" sometimes suggested for discretionary purchases?
choices: Never spend more than 1% of your net worth on any single item
Tip at least 1% on every purchase to support workers
Save exactly 1% of each paycheck before spending anything
If a non-essential item costs more than 1% of your monthly income, wait at least a day before buying it
explanation: The 1% rule is a simple impulse-control habit: if a discretionary item costs more than 1% of your gross monthly income, you pause — often a full day — before deciding, while cheaper items can be bought without the wait. Scaling the threshold to your income means the same rule fits very different budgets. Like other cooling-off habits, it converts a snap decision into a deliberate one without banning the purchase.
source: Ramit Sethi — I Will Teach You to Be Rich (popularized money rule)

prompt: What is "shrinkflation"?
choices: When a product's package gets smaller while the price stays the same, so you get less for your money
When prices fall because a product has shrunk in popularity
When a store shrinks its floor space to cut costs
When inflation slows down and prices stop rising
explanation: Shrinkflation is when a manufacturer quietly reduces the size or quantity of a product while keeping the price unchanged, so the cost per unit rises even though the sticker looks the same. Because the headline price doesn't move, the increase is easy to miss unless you compare the unit price or net weight. Checking the price per ounce or per item — not just the package price — is the way to catch it.
source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics — explainer on package downsizing and CPI

prompt: How does the "endowment effect" influence spending and decluttering decisions?
choices: It makes people value things they already own more highly than identical things they don't, so they overpay to keep them and resist letting go
It causes people to undervalue gifts they receive relative to items they buy
It makes shoppers prefer cheaper store brands over name brands
It describes spending a windfall faster than earned income
explanation: The endowment effect is the bias by which merely owning something raises its perceived value, so people demand more to give up an item than they'd have paid to acquire it. In spending, it underlies free-trial-then-keep tactics, reluctance to return or resell purchases, and clinging to subscriptions or clutter you'd never buy again today. The corrective question is whether you would buy the item now at its current price — not how it feels to part with it.
source: Kahneman, Knetsch & Thaler — Journal of Economic Perspectives (1991)

prompt: A rideshare that normally costs $18 is showing a 1.8x surge (dynamic-pricing) multiplier. How much extra are you paying versus the normal fare?
choices: $1.80 extra
$32.40 extra
$14.40 extra
$18.00 extra
explanation: A surge multiplier scales the whole fare, so the total is 18 × 1.8 = $32.40, and the extra over the normal price is 32.40 − 18 = $14.40. Dynamic pricing raises the price when demand spikes, so the premium — here 80% on top of the base — is what you'd avoid by waiting for surge to fade or choosing another option. The $32.40 distractor reports the total fare, not the extra.

prompt: A cereal box was 18 oz for $4.50. After shrinkflation, it's 14 oz for the same $4.50. By what percent did the price per ounce effectively rise?
choices: About 4.0% higher per ounce
About 22.2% higher per ounce
0% — the price didn't change
About 28.6% higher per ounce
explanation: Compare price per ounce: before, 4.50 ÷ 18 = $0.250/oz; after, 4.50 ÷ 14 ≈ $0.3214/oz. The percent increase is (0.3214 − 0.250) ÷ 0.250 ≈ 0.286, or about 28.6%. Holding the shelf price constant while cutting the quantity is a hidden price hike that only the unit price reveals — the '0%' answer is exactly the illusion shrinkflation relies on. The 22.2% distractor is just the size reduction (4 of 18 oz), not the per-ounce price change.

prompt: An extended warranty costs $120. The only covered repair would cost $300, and you estimate a 25% chance you'll actually need it. What is the warranty's expected payout, and how does it compare with the price?
choices: $300 expected payout, far above the price
$120 expected payout, exactly breaking even
$225 expected payout, well above the price
$75 expected payout, which is $45 below the $120 price
explanation: Expected payout = probability of a claim × repair cost = 0.25 × 300 = $75. You pay $120 for an expected $75 of coverage, so the warranty costs about $45 more than it's statistically worth — that gap is the seller's margin and is why most extended warranties favor the seller. Expected value won't capture a catastrophic risk you truly can't absorb, but for a modest, affordable repair like this, self-insuring is usually the better bet.
source: Consumer Reports — guidance on extended warranties

prompt: Durable boots cost $250 and last 10 years; a cheap pair costs $45 but lasts only 1.5 years. On a cost-per-year basis, which is cheaper?
choices: The cheap boots, at $45/yr
$25/yr for the durable boots, beating $30/yr for the cheap pair
They tie at about $27.50/yr
The durable boots, at $250/yr
explanation: Cost per year = price ÷ years of service: durable is 250 ÷ 10 = $25/yr, while cheap is 45 ÷ 1.5 = $30/yr. Spreading each price across its lifespan shows the pricier 'buy once' pair is actually cheaper per year, but only because its longer life more than offsets the higher sticker. If the cheap pair lasted nearly as long, the premium wouldn't pay off, so the discipline is to estimate service life and compute cost per year before paying up.

prompt: You spend $400/month on dining. A card paying 4% cashback on dining versus a flat 1% card earns how much extra cashback per year — assuming you'd spend the same either way and pay in full?
choices: $48 extra per year
$192 extra per year
$16 extra per year
$144 extra per year
explanation: Cashback = spend × rate. At 4%: 400 × 0.04 × 12 = $192/yr; at 1% flat: 400 × 0.01 × 12 = $48/yr; the extra is 192 − 48 = $144/yr. Matching a high-rate category card to where you actually spend is the core of cashback optimization — but it only pays if you'd have spent the money anyway and clear the balance, since one cycle of ~20% interest would erase the gain. The $192 distractor is the gross 4% cashback, not the extra over the flat card.

prompt: In retail, what does "gift-card breakage" refer to, and why does it matter to a careful spender?
choices: The fee a retailer charges to issue a physical gift card
The portion of gift-card value that is never redeemed, which is pure profit for the seller and lost money for the buyer
The penalty for reselling a gift card below face value
The physical damage that voids a gift card's balance
explanation: Breakage is the value loaded onto gift cards that customers never spend — forgotten balances, lost cards, and stranded small remainders — which the issuer ultimately keeps as profit. For the buyer it's money paid but never used, effectively a gift to the retailer, and it's why prepaid balances are worth tracking and spending down promptly. Treating gift cards (and store credit) like cash you've already spent helps you avoid quietly donating the leftovers.
source: Mercator Advisory Group — gift-card breakage industry estimates
